title: How much do language models memorize?
author: John X. Morris et al
description: This paper introduces a novel method for quantifying language model memorization by separating it into unintended memorization (dataset-specific information) and generalization (learned patterns about the data-generating process). The authors use Kolmogorov complexity approximated through compression rates to measure memorization at the instance level, estimating that GPT-family models have a capacity of approximately 3.6 bits-per-parameter. Through experiments on both synthetic random data and real text, they demonstrate that models memorize until reaching capacity, after which they begin generalizing—a transition point that coincides with the double descent phenomenon. The work produces scaling laws relating model capacity and dataset size to membership inference success, predicting that modern large language models trained on massive datasets are largely immune to membership inference attacks on average datapoints.

title: Diffusion Models with Learned Adaptive Noise
author: Subham Sekhar Sahoo et al
description: This paper introduces MULAN (Multivariate Learned Adaptive Noise), a method that learns the diffusion process in diffusion models rather than using a fixed noise schedule. The key technical innovation is a multivariate noise schedule that applies different noise rates to each pixel and adapts based on input context through auxiliary latent variables, which the authors show makes the ELBO objective non-invariant to the noise process choice. By jointly optimizing both the forward diffusion process and reverse denoising model, MULAN achieves state-of-the-art density estimation on CIFAR-10 (2.55 BPD) and ImageNet-32 (3.67 BPD). The method also reduces training time by 50% compared to previous approaches while maintaining or improving sample quality.

title: The GAN is dead; long live the GAN! A Modern Baseline GAN
author: Yiwen Huang et al
description: This paper introduces R3GAN, a simplified GAN baseline that addresses training instability and mode collapse through a regularized relativistic loss function. The authors prove that combining relativistic pairing GAN (RpGAN) with R1 and R2 gradient penalties provides local convergence guarantees, eliminating the need for ad-hoc training tricks. With this stable loss, they modernize the network architecture by replacing StyleGAN2's outdated backbone with a clean ResNet-based design incorporating ConvNeXt principles like grouped convolutions and inverted bottlenecks. The resulting model achieves competitive or superior FID scores compared to StyleGAN2 and recent diffusion models across FFHQ, ImageNet, CIFAR-10, and Stacked MNIST datasets, while requiring only a single forward pass for generation.

title: Encoder-Decoder Diffusion Language Models for Efficient Training and Inference
author: Marianne Arriola et al
description: E2D2 proposes an encoder-decoder architecture for discrete diffusion models that separates the computation of representing clean tokens (encoder) from denoising corrupted tokens (lightweight decoder), enabling the expensive encoder to be called once per block while the cheap decoder is called T times for iterative denoising. For inference, this architecture achieves significantly faster generation than decoder-only diffusion models (e.g., ~75% speedup on summarization) while maintaining or improving sample quality. For training block diffusion models, E2D2 halves the computational cost compared to decoder-only BD3-LMs by processing clean tokens through the encoder and noisy tokens through the decoder in parallel, rather than processing 2L tokens through the full model. The paper demonstrates state-of-the-art quality-throughput trade-offs on summarization, translation, and mathematical reasoning tasks, extending the Pareto frontier beyond existing diffusion models.

title: DiRL: An Efficient Post-Training Framework for Diffusion Language Models
author: Ying Zhu et al
description: DiRL introduces an efficient post-training framework for diffusion language models by tightly integrating FlexAttention-accelerated blockwise training with LMDeploy-optimized inference, enabling fast online model updates without repeated checkpoint loading. The framework implements DiPO, the first unbiased GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) for diffusion models, which computes token-level importance sampling ratios using the efficient blockwise structure where generation is autoregressive between blocks but parallel within blocks. FlexAttention handles the complex attention masks required by blockwise models (where both prompts and outputs are repeated blockwise) more efficiently than standard FlashAttention, achieving nearly 6× training speedup. The system eliminates I/O bottlenecks by deploying models in an API server with in-place parameter updates instead of repeatedly saving/loading checkpoints, reducing per-step latency by 2.5× overall and enabling practical RL training on diffusion language models.

title: Janus: Decoupling Visual Encoding for Unified Multimodel Understanding and Generation
author: Chengyue Wu et al
description: The paper introduces Janus, an autoregressive multimodal framework that unifies multimodal understanding and image generation while explicitly decoupling visual encoding for the two tasks. Its key insight is that understanding and generation require fundamentally different visual representations—high-level semantic features versus fine-grained spatial detail—and forcing a single vision encoder to serve both leads to systematic trade-offs. Janus resolves this by using two specialized visual encoders (with lightweight adaptors) feeding into a single shared transformer, preserving architectural simplicity while increasing flexibility and extensibility. Empirically, this design outperforms prior unified models and even matches or exceeds some task-specific models on both multimodal understanding and visual generation benchmarks, despite using fewer parameters.

title: Auxiliary-Loss-Free Load Balancing Strategy for Mixture-of-Experts
author: Lean Wang et al
description: The paper identifies a fundamental drawback of standard MoE load balancing: auxiliary losses enforce balance but inject interference gradients that conflict with the language modeling objective, creating an unavoidable trade-off between balance and performance. To remove this conflict, it proposes Loss-Free Balancing, which controls expert load by adding expert-wise biases to routing scores before top-K selection, rather than by optimizing an auxiliary loss. These biases are dynamically updated based on recent expert load, suppressing overloaded experts and promoting underused ones, while remaining outside the gradient path and preserving causal training. The key reasoning is that explicit control of routing decisions can achieve stable, balanced expert utilization without gradient interference or future-token leakage, yielding both better load balance and improved model performance.

title: Let the Expert Stick to His Last: Expert-Specialized Fine-Tuning for Sparse Architectural Large Language Models
author: Zihan Wang et al
description: The paper observes that in MoE LLMs, a given downstream task routes tokens to a highly concentrated subset of experts, and different tasks concentrate on different expert subsets, implying strong task-specific expert specialization. Based on this, they propose Expert-Specialized Fine-Tuning (ESFT): estimate per-layer expert relevance from a small probe sample of task data using either average gate value or token selection ratio, then fine-tune only the experts whose cumulative relevance exceeds a threshold 𝑝 while freezing all other experts and (optionally) other modules. The reasoning is that updating only task-relevant (non-shared) experts preserves the MoE system’s specialization and reduces interference/forgetting that happens when unrelated experts and shared parameters are trained, so you keep general ability while still improving the target task. They further argue ESFT works best with fine-grained expert segmentation (many small experts), because it makes it easier to select a task-appropriate expert combination at fixed compute, whereas coarser experts blur specialization and weaken the benefit of selective tuning.

title: Training and Inference on Any-Order Autoregressive Models the Right Way
author: Andy Shih et al
description: This paper improves Any-Order Autoregressive Models (AO-ARMs) by addressing two key deficiencies in their training. First, the authors show that previous AO-ARMs redundantly define the same distribution multiple ways by training on all possible variable orderings, so they propose training on a smaller set of univariate conditionals that still supports efficient arbitrary conditional inference. Second, they align the training objective with inference by upweighting univariate conditionals that are evaluated more frequently during inference, based on analyzing the recursive decomposition of marginal queries through a binary lattice structure. Their method MAC (Mask-tuned Arbitrary Conditional Models) achieves state-of-the-art likelihoods across text, image, and tabular domains by reducing redundancy while maintaining tractability. The key insight is reinterpreting AO-ARMs as recursive decomposition over masks rather than ensembles of orderings, enabling a more efficient decomposition protocol that concentrates training on frequently-used conditionals.

title: Any-Order GPT as Masked Diffusion Model: Decoupling Formulation and Architecture
author: Shuchen Xue et al
description: Any-Order GPT as Masked Diffusion Model argues that prior AR vs masked-diffusion comparisons are confounded because AR is usually decoder-only with causal attention while masked diffusion is usually encoder-only with full attention, so they implement masked diffusion (equivalently any-order autoregression) inside a decoder-only GPT-style model (“AO-GPT”) to separate formulation from architecture. Holding architecture fixed, they show uniform any-order training converges slower than standard left-to-right, and that many random permutations are weak learning signals for language; mixing in a small amount of left-to-right orders (e.g., 10%) substantially improves both left-to-right and any-order performance, suggesting the objective should weight orders non-uniformly toward language’s natural structure. Holding formulation fixed (MDM/AO-AR), they analyze the architectural trade-off: encoder-only models predict order-invariant conditionals over token subsets (a smaller conditional space) and achieve better perplexity, while decoder-only any-order models must model many more order-dependent conditionals but can close the perplexity gap via context-order ensembling. Crucially, decoder-only MDMs enable much faster sampling because causal attention plus KV-cache and efficient unmasking let generation scale linearly in sequence length, yielding large practical speedups (reported ~25×) with comparable generation quality when using appropriate temperature/top-p annealing.

title: Diffusion-LM Improves Controllable Text Generation
author: Xiang Lisa Li et al
description: Diffusion-LM is a non-autoregressive language model that generates text by iteratively denoising a sequence of continuous latent vectors into word embeddings, producing a hierarchy of intermediate representations. The model is trained end-to-end with learned token embeddings and a modified diffusion objective that predicts clean representations directly, combined with rounding and clamping techniques to reliably map continuous vectors back to discrete words. For controllable generation, Diffusion-LM applies gradient-based guidance from external classifiers directly to the continuous latent variables at each diffusion step, explicitly balancing fluency and control. This allows effective fine-grained and global controls—such as syntactic structure, part-of-speech patterns, length, and infilling—that are difficult to achieve with left-to-right autoregressive decoding.

title: Ssd LM: Semi-autoregressive Simplex-based Diffusion Language Model for Text Generation and Modular Control
author: Xiaochuang Han et al
description: SSD-LM proposes a diffusion language model that generates text semi-autoregressively: it produces the next block of B tokens left-to-right, but within each block it denoises iteratively so tokens can be refined using bidirectional context, which also lets decoding stop at any length by generating more blocks as needed. The second key technique is simplex-based diffusion: instead of diffusing in an embedding/latent space, each token is mapped to a fixed “almost one-hot” logit vector over the vocabulary (a simplex after softmax), Gaussian noise is added to these logits across timesteps, and a Transformer is trained with an NLL objective to recover the original tokens given the noisy simplex and the left context. At inference, SSD-LM starts from pure Gaussian noise for a block and repeatedly predicts token distributions, then applies a logits-projection step (greedy / top-p sample / multi-hot nucleus) to convert the predicted distribution back into an “almost one-hot” logit representation before continuing reverse diffusion, which the authors found works better for simplex diffusion than vanilla DDPM updates. Because the model’s state is always a vocabulary-level distribution using standard subword tokenization, SSD-LM enables modular classifier guidance: at each denoising step it adds a gradient from any off-the-shelf attribute classifier that shares the tokenizer to steer generation (e.g., sentiment) without retraining the classifier or the LM.

title: Diffusion LLMs Can Do Faster-Than-AR Inference via Discrete Diffusion Forcing
author: Xu Wang et al
description: This paper introduces Discrete Diffusion Forcing (D2F), an AR–diffusion hybrid training and decoding paradigm that enables faster-than-autoregressive inference for diffusion LLMs by combining block-wise causal generation (for exact KV cache reuse) with inter-block parallel decoding. The key technical idea is to train dLLMs to denoise blocks with monotonically increasing mask ratios, so later blocks can be predicted from partially denoised prefixes, implemented via an asymmetric distillation objective from a bidirectional dLLM teacher to a block-causal student. At inference time, a pipelined parallel decoding algorithm with dynamic block activation and confidence-based token admission trades off speed and quality while preserving cache correctness. Empirically, D2F turns existing dLLMs (LLaDA, Dream) into the first open-source diffusion models that outperform similarly sized AR LLMs in throughput (up to 2.5× faster than LLaMA-3/Qwen-2.5 and >50× over vanilla dLLMs) while maintaining comparable performance on math and code benchmarks.

title: DiffuSeq: Sequence to Sequence Text Generation with Diffusion Models
author: Shansan Gong et al
description: DIFFUSEQ introduces a classifier-free diffusion framework for sequence-to-sequence conditional text generation by embedding source+target tokens into a continuous space and applying a partial noising forward process that corrupts only the target portion while keeping the source as an anchor/condition. In the reverse process, a Transformer denoiser predicts the clean target representations using full-context attention, plus an anchoring + rounding procedure during sampling (round to nearby token embeddings and explicitly re-insert the original source embedding each step) to keep conditioning stable. The paper adds practical training/inference tricks—importance sampling over diffusion timesteps (to focus learning where loss is large), DDIM-style step reduction for faster sampling, and Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) reranking that leverages diffusion’s naturally diverse candidates to improve final quality. Empirically on multiple Seq2Seq tasks (dialogue, question generation, simplification, paraphrase), DIFFUSEQ matches or beats strong AR/NAR and even fine-tuned PLM baselines while producing higher sentence-level diversity, and it provides a theoretical connection framing DIFFUSEQ as a continuous-space extension of iterative non-autoregressive generation (helping explain its quality/diversity behavior).

title: Rewriting Pre-Training Data Boosts LLM Performance in Math and Code
author: Kazuki Fujii et al
description: The SwallowCode paper. This paper argues that LLM performance in math and code is bottlenecked by pre-training data quality and shows that rewriting public corpora is more effective than filtering alone. It introduces SwallowCode (16.1B tokens) and SwallowMath (2.3B tokens), constructed via a transform-and-retain pipeline that combines rule-based cleaning with large-scale LLM-driven rewriting to enforce style, self-containment, and semantic quality. For code, the pipeline applies syntax checks, pylint filtering, then two LLM stages—Style-Guided Code Rewriting (SGCR) and Self-Contained Optimization Rewriting (SCOR)—which together deliver the largest gains by improving readability, resolving dependencies, and upgrading algorithms. With a fixed 50B-token continual pre-training budget on Llama-3.1-8B, the rewritten datasets yield large improvements (e.g., +17 pass@1 on HumanEval, +12.4 on GSM8K), demonstrating that systematic rewriting of existing data can outperform exclusionary filtering and synthetic-only approaches.

title: Open Thoughts: Data Recipes for Reasoning Models
author: Etash Guha et al
description: OpenThoughts (v3) is an open-data recipe for reasoning SFT: they run 1,000+ controlled ablations over a data-generation pipeline (question sourcing → mixing top sources → LLM-based question filtering → dedup + multi-sample teacher answers → teacher choice) and then scale the best choices to OpenThoughts3-1.2M (850k math / 250k code / 100k science). Implementation-wise, the strongest recipe surprisingly keeps few high-quality sources (often top 1–2), uses LLM-based filters like difficulty/response-length rather than embeddings/fastText, scales mainly by sampling multiple teacher answers per question (e.g., up to 16×), and largely skips answer filtering because it didn’t beat “keep everything” in their compute-controlled tests. A notable finding is teacher selection: QwQ-32B works better as a distillation teacher than DeepSeek-R1 for their pipeline even though it can score worse on some target benchmarks, so they use QwQ-32B to annotate the final dataset. The benefit is a reproducible, public alternative to proprietary reasoning corpora: fine-tuning Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct on OpenThoughts3-1.2M yields OpenThinker3-7B, reported at about 53% AIME 2025, 51% LiveCodeBench (06/24–01/25), and 54% GPQA Diamond, substantially ahead of DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, with datasets/models released on openthoughts.ai

title: Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization
author: Ilya Loshchilov et al
description: Decoupled Weight Decay (the “AdamW/SGDW” paper) shows that L2 regularization is only equivalent to weight decay for plain SGD (up to a learning-rate rescaling), but not for adaptive optimizers like Adam because Adam’s per-parameter normalization also rescales the L2 gradient, making regularization uneven across parameters with different historical gradient magnitudes. Their implementation fix is simple: apply weight decay as a separate parameter shrink step (e.g., θ ← θ − α * update_from_loss and independently θ ← θ * (1 − αλ)), rather than adding λθ into the loss gradient—this yields AdamW (and SGDW) with essentially the same compute as the original optimizer. A key benefit is that this decouples the best weight-decay setting from the learning-rate setting, making hyperparameter tuning less entangled and improving training stability across schedules (they particularly highlight cosine annealing / warm restarts as helpful). Empirically, this decoupling substantially improves Adam’s generalization, often closing much of the gap to SGD+momentum on image classification (e.g., CIFAR/ImageNet32x32), which is why “AdamW” became the default in many modern training recipes.

title: RLP: Reinforcement as a Pretraining Objective
author: Ali Hatamizadeh et al
description: RLP (“Reinforcement Learning Pre-training”) changes pretraining by sampling a short chain-of-thought as an action before each next-token prediction, then rewarding that thought by its information gain: the log-likelihood improvement on the observed next token when conditioning on (context + thought) versus a no-think EMA teacher baseline (computed under teacher forcing). Implementation-wise, they use multiple thought rollouts per context, form group-relative advantages, and apply a PPO/GRPO-like clipped surrogate update that updates only the thought tokens (treating the reward as a stop-grad), while the EMA baseline is updated slowly (e.g., τ≈0.999) to stabilize training and reduce reward hacking. The key benefit is a verifier-free, dense, position-wise reward that works on ordinary web-scale text (no task-specific graders, no sparse success signals), encouraging “think-then-predict” behavior during pretraining rather than relegating RL to post-training. Empirically, they report sizable and persistent reasoning gains: on Qwen3-1.7B-Base, RLP lifts an 8-benchmark math+science average by about +19% and the improvements compound after identical post-training, and scaling to Nemotron-Nano-12B-v2 raises overall average from 42.81% to 61.32% with especially large science-reasoning boosts.

title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
author: Jordan Hoffman et al
description: This paper re-derives the compute-optimal tradeoff between model size and training data by training over 400 transformer language models across a wide range of parameters and token counts, showing that many existing large models are substantially undertrained. Using three independent estimation methods, the authors find that under a fixed compute budget, model parameters and training tokens should scale in roughly equal proportions, in contrast to prior work that favored scaling parameters more aggressively. They validate this prediction by training Chinchilla, a 70B-parameter model on 1.4T tokens at the same compute budget as Gopher, and show that it consistently outperforms much larger models across diverse benchmarks. The results imply that future scaling gains will depend more on increasing high-quality training data and yield practical benefits through smaller, more efficient models with lower inference and fine-tuning costs.

title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
author: Jared Kaplan et al
description: Kaplan et al. (2020) empirically demonstrate that Transformer language-model cross-entropy loss follows simple power-law scaling with non-embedding model parameters, dataset size, and training compute across many orders of magnitude. They show that performance depends primarily on scale rather than architectural details, and that overfitting behavior collapses to a predictable function of the ratio between model size and data size. By modeling learning curves and batch-size effects, they derive a unified framework relating loss to model size and training time. Using this framework, they argue that compute-efficient training favors very large models trained on comparatively modest amounts of data and stopped well before full convergence.

title: BroRL: Scaling Reinforcement Learning via Broadened Exploration
author: Jian Hu et al
description: BRORL introduces rollout-size scaling as a new axis for RLVR by broadening exploration per prompt, arguing that step-based scaling saturates due to insufficient coverage of the action space rather than fundamental limits of RL. Its main theoretical contribution is a mass-balance analysis of a single RL update, which decomposes the change in total correct-token probability mass into (i) two always-nonnegative sampled terms—rewarding sampled correct tokens and penalizing sampled incorrect ones—and (ii) an unsampled coupling term that can be negative but provably vanishes as the number of rollouts increases, guaranteeing monotonic expansion of correct probability mass at large rollout sizes. On the algorithmic side, BroRL is implemented on top of a clipped PPO-style RLVR objective, using decoupled advantage normalization (group-level baselining followed by batch normalization) and dynamic sampling, with the key change being a large per-prompt rollout count that stabilizes PPO updates by reducing variance and suppressing harmful unsampled effects.

title: Unified Multimodal Discrete Diffusion
author: Alexander Swerdlow et al
description: UniDisc is the first unified multimodal discrete diffusion model that jointly generates text and images by randomly masking tokens and iteratively denoising them through a shared vocabulary, rather than using fixed autoregressive token ordering. The model unifies previously disparate design choices across text and image domains by applying an absorbing transition matrix with masked tokens, discrete noise schedules, and weighted cross-entropy loss uniformly across both modalities. UniDisc incorporates classifier-free guidance by randomly masking entire modalities during training and uses confidence-based sampling during inference to iteratively unmask the most confident predictions across diffusion steps. The framework naturally supports joint image-text inpainting without fine-tuning, demonstrates superior performance on multimodal retrieval tasks through variable denoising steps, and enables zero-shot image editing by denoising corrupted image-text pairs using the model's likelihood as a scoring function.

title: dInfer: An Efficient Inference Framework for Diffusion Language Models
author: Yuxin Ma et al
description: This framework proposes several algorithmic innovations to improve inference efficiency. Iteration smoothing retains token representations from the previous denoising iteration and fuses them with the next iteration's embeddings, enabling cross-iteration information flow that enriches contextual cues available to the model and improves token confidence predictions while mitigating performance degradation from KV-cache deployment. Hierarchical decoding is a novel decoding strategy that recursively partitions masked token spans while ensuring at least one token is decoded per region, reducing local dependencies between tokens and improving decoding efficiency without requiring model retraining. Credit decoding serves as an alternative strategy that accumulates historical confidence scores as "credits" and preferentially commits tokens with consistently stable predictions across iterations, enhancing reliability by favoring tokens with proven stability rather than single-iteration confidence thresholds. The vicinity KV-cache refresh method addresses the challenge of bidirectional attention by selectively updating a small window of tokens adjacent to the current decoding block while reusing cache for distant tokens, then performing a full cache update once a block is fully decoded to ensure global consistency, thereby balancing computational cost with accuracy.

title: Scaling Data-Constrained Language Models
author: Niklas Muennighoff et al
description: This paper investigates scaling language models when unique training data is limited, a critical concern as high-quality internet text approaches exhaustion. The authors conduct over 400 training runs to empirically study how repeated data affects model performance and propose a new data-constrained scaling law that extends the Chinchilla scaling framework by accounting for the diminishing value of repeated tokens through exponential decay formulations. They demonstrate that training for up to 4 epochs of repeated data incurs negligible performance penalties compared to unique data, but returns diminish rapidly beyond that point, with an estimated half-life of approximately 15 epochs. Finally, they explore complementary strategies including code augmentation and relaxed data filtering, finding that mixing in code can effectively double the available training tokens while maintaining performance on natural language tasks.

title: Diffusion Beats Autoregressive in Data-Constrained Settings
author: Mihir Prabhudesai et al
description: The paper systematically demonstrates that masked diffusion models substantially outperform autoregressive models in data-constrained settings where training involves repeated passes over limited data, contrary to prior work suggesting diffusion requires 16× more compute. Through fitting scaling laws across hundreds of models, the authors show diffusion models exhibit an effective epoch half-life (R*_D) of ~500 compared to ~30 for AR models, enabling them to benefit from repeated data for far longer without overfitting. The paper derives a closed-form power-law expression (Ccrit(U) ∝ U^2.174) that predicts the critical compute threshold at which diffusion begins to outperform AR as a function of dataset size, providing practitioners with actionable guidance on when to use each paradigm. The authors attribute diffusion's superior sample efficiency to its implicit training over diverse token orderings acting as data augmentation, which they validate experimentally by showing AR models trained on multiple random permutations approach diffusion performance.

title: Training Diffusion Language Models at Scale using Autoregressive Models
author: Radical Numerics Inc.
description: RND1-Base introduces a simple, single-stage continual pretraining (SCP) recipe for converting autoregressive models into large-scale diffusion language models without complex attention-mask annealing strategies, achieving a 30B-parameter MoE model trained on 500B tokens that outperforms smaller diffusion models. The paper demonstrates that critical batch size for diffusion models extends beyond 8M tokens, indicating diffusion models can effectively utilize larger batches than previously thought. To preserve autoregressive knowledge during conversion, the authors employ layer-specific learning rates—maintaining higher rates for attention parameters while using near-zero rates for non-attention (FFN/embedding) parameters—which empirically prevents catastrophic forgetting of pretrained knowledge. RND1-Base achieves state-of-the-art performance among diffusion language models on reasoning, math, and coding benchmarks while establishing a foundation for scalable diffusion model training.

title: LLaDA2.0: Scaling Up Diffusion Language Models to 100B
author: Tiwei Bei et al
description: LLaDA2.0 introduces a scalable method for converting pre-trained auto-regressive models into discrete diffusion language models at frontier scale (up to 100B parameters), avoiding costly training from scratch while preserving linguistic knowledge. The paper proposes a novel Warmup-Stable-Decay (WSD) training strategy that progressively expands block sizes during continual pre-training, enabling smooth transition from left-to-right generation to bidirectional denoising while improving data efficiency. To address practical deployment, the authors develop post-training techniques including complementary masking for data utilization, confidence-aware parallel training for efficient inference, and a reformulated DPO objective adapted for diffusion models. The resulting models (LLaDA2.0-mini at 16B and LLaDA2.0-flash at 100B) achieve competitive performance with auto-regressive baselines while demonstrating particular strengths in structured generation tasks like code and tool use.

title: CANDI: Hybrid Discrete-Continuous Diffusion Models
author: Patrick Pynadeth et al
description: CANDI explains why continuous diffusion struggles on discrete data by introducing token identifiability, splitting Gaussian corruption into (i) discrete identity corruption (argmax flips) and (ii) continuous rank degradation (how often incorrect tokens outrank the true one), and showing these two scales mismatch badly as vocabulary size grows ("temporal dissonance"). It then proposes a hybrid noising kernel that decouples discrete masking and continuous Gaussian noise, so both forms of corruption follow controlled, roughly linear schedules and the model can learn discrete conditional structure and a continuous score function at the same time. This hybrid design enables practical benefits: better low-NFE text generation frontiers than masked diffusion and straightforward classifier guidance via gradient addition using off-the-shelf classifiers, without diffusion-specific heads. Compared to CADD, which augments a masked diffusion LM with a continuous latent channel mainly to fix the [MASK] information void and improve mode coverage/quality in a largely discrete-first framework, CANDI is more theory-driven, explicitly targets the Gaussian-vs-discrete mismatch at large vocabularies, and uses that analysis to design a hybrid process whose corruption schedules and inference algorithm are built around token identifiability rather than just semantic hinting.

title: Continuously Augmented Discrete Diffusion model for Categorical Generative Modeling
author: Huangjie Zheng et al
description: Continuously Augmented Discrete Diffusion (CADD) extends masked discrete diffusion by giving every masked token a paired continuous latent that decays gradually instead of collapsing immediately to the absorbing [MASK] state. This continuous trajectory preserves graded semantic information about the original token, removing the “information void” that standard discrete diffusion models suffer from. At inference, the model can draw one or more continuous samples to trade off between mode-seeking (precise) and mode-covering (diverse) behavior—something masked diffusion cannot do. Overall, CADD keeps the same architecture and training simplicity as standard DLMs but adds a continuous augmentation that yields more stable scaling with steps and consistently higher quality across text, image, and code generation.

title: Test-Time Scaling of Diffusion Models via Noise Trajectory Search
author: Vignav Ramesh et al
description: They reframe diffusion sampling as a control problem, where each timestep’s injected noise is treated as an action that can be optimized rather than drawn randomly. To approximate this high-dimensional search efficiently, they replace the full Markov decision process with a sequence of independent contextual bandits. They then introduce an ε-greedy noise-trajectory search algorithm that mixes global exploration of the Gaussian noise space with local hill-climbing around promising noise candidates, using Tweedie’s formula to score each candidate without running a full rollout. This produces a practical, gradient-free method for optimizing arbitrary test-time rewards by selectively choosing per-step noise during inference.

title: Set Block Decoding is a Language Model Inference Accelerator
author: Itai Gat et al
description: This paper proposes Set Block Decoding (SBD), a decoding paradigm that unifies next-token prediction and masked token prediction within a single transformer, enabling parallel sampling of non-consecutive future tokens. It shows how to train SBD by fine-tuning standard NTP models—without architectural changes or extra training hyperparameters—so the model retains AR capability while gaining set-parallel decoding. For inference, SBD integrates entropy-bounded set selection (e.g., EB-Sampler) and remains compatible with exact KV-caching, letting blocks be decoded in arbitrary order and then cached. The authors analyze latency with a roofline model and argue that reductions in function evaluations translate closely to wall-clock gains, providing a practical, solver-agnostic route to accelerate LM decoding without sacrificing baseline behavior.

title: Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking
author: Heli Ben-Hamu et al
description: This paper introduces EB-Sampler (Entropy Bounded Sampler), an adaptive sampling method for masked diffusion models (MDMs) that improves efficiency without degrading generation quality. It builds on the observation that multiple masked tokens can often be predicted with low entropy and unmasked simultaneously with minimal error. EB-Sampler dynamically determines how many tokens to unmask per step by bounding the joint dependence among token predictions through an entropy-based criterion. The authors provide theoretical grounding showing how EB-Sampler fits within a broader family of adaptive multi-token samplers and demonstrate that it yields 2–3× faster sampling on language, coding, and reasoning benchmarks compared to standard MDM samplers.

title: DLER: Doing Length pEnalty Right - Incentivizing More Intelligence per Token via Reinforcement Learning
author: Shih-Yang Liu et al
description: DLER re-examines length penalties for reasoning LMs and shows that the accuracy loss seen with simple truncation comes from optimization, not the penalty itself. They identify three culprits—biased advantage estimates under GRPO (from high reward variance), entropy collapse, and sparse signals—and fix them with a recipe: batch-wise reward normalization, higher/decoupled clipping, dynamic sampling (curriculum), plus the plain truncation penalty. This yields state-of-the-art accuracy–efficiency trade-offs—~70% shorter outputs while matching or improving accuracy—and enables superior test-time parallel scaling (higher Pass@K at lower latency). They also add Difficulty-Aware DLER (adaptive truncation by problem hardness) and an update-selective weight-merging method that preserves baseline accuracy while retaining concise reasoning when full RL data isn’t available. Awesome work Shih-Yang! Glad to see it out!

title: ProRL: Prolonged Reinforcement Learning Expands Reasoning Boundaries in Large Language Models
author: Mingjie Liu et al
description: The ProRL paper proposes Prolonged Reinforcement Learning (ProRL) as a method to expand reasoning abilities in large language models beyond what is accessible via supervised training or short RL runs. Instead of relying only on reward-model alignment, ProRL uses verifiable rewards (e.g., correctness in math/code), Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with entropy-preserving techniques, KL-regularization with periodic reference policy resets, and a diverse multi-domain training set (math, code, STEM, logic, instruction following). The authors show that extended RL (over 2k+ steps) continues to yield improvements, avoids entropy collapse, and enables models to discover new reasoning strategies not present in the base distribution. Their resulting model, Nemotron-Research-Reasoning-Qwen-1.5B, significantly outperforms both its base model and larger specialized models across a broad set of reasoning benchmarks.

title: A Spectral Condition for Feature Learning
author: Greg Yang et al
description: The paper introduces a spectral scaling condition that ensures feature learning in neural networks at large width by requiring the spectral norms of weight matrices and their updates to scale like √(fan-out/fan-in). This condition provides a simpler explanation and implementation of maximal update parametrization (µP), contrasting with heuristic Frobenius- or entry-based scalings that can fail to preserve feature learning. The authors show through linear algebra arguments, extensions to nonlinear and minibatch settings, and empirical results that spectral scaling preserves nontrivial feature evolution, whereas standard and neural tangent parameterizations do not. Overall, the work gives a unifying principle for hyperparameter scaling that applies across architectures and optimizers, making feature learning analysis more transparent.

title: Scalable Second Order Optimization for Deep Learning
author: Rohan Anil et al
description: The paper addresses the challenge of making second-order optimization practical for deep learning, focusing on a scalable implementation of the Shampoo algorithm (a Kronecker-factored full-matrix AdaGrad variant). The authors propose several system-level and algorithmic improvements, including pipelining computations across CPUs and accelerators, efficient iterative methods for computing matrix inverse roots, block-wise preconditioning for large layers, and delayed preconditioner updates.

title: Scalable Optimization in the Modular Norm
author: Tim Large et al
description: The paper introduces the modular norm, a recursive architecture-aware norm that generalizes “natural norms” for layers to the entire neural network. By normalizing weight updates in this norm, optimizers like Adam and SGD achieve scale-invariant learning rates across width and depth, eliminating the need for optimizer-specific correction factors. Theoretically, the authors prove that gradients of networks built from well-behaved modules are Lipschitz-continuous in the modular norm, with sharpness constants admitting simple recursive formulas.

title: On the distance between two neural networks and the stability of learning
author: Jeremy Bernstein et al
description: The paper proposes a "deep relative trust" distance that upper-bounds relative changes in a network’s function and layer-to-output Jacobians by a product of layerwise relative parameter perturbations, explaining quasi-exponential breakdown with depth. Using this model, the authors derive a descent lemma and the Frobenius-matched gradient descent optimizer (Fromage), which scales each layer’s step to a fixed relative change and adds a norm-preserving prefactor (unlike LARS). Empirically, they show gradient/loss sensitivity grows quasi-exponentially with perturbation size in deep MLPs, and that Fromage trains very deep MLPs and performs competitively on CIFAR-10, ImageNet, GANs, and a Transformer with minimal LR tuning (often η=0.01). Overall, the work argues that layerwise relative updates—rather than Euclidean trust regions—better capture deep nets’ geometry and can reduce learning-rate sensitivity in practice.

title: Rotational Equilibrium: How Weight Decay Balances Learning Across Neural Networks
author: Atli Kosson et al
description: This paper investigates how weight decay affects the optimization dynamics of individual neurons in neural networks, revealing that it creates "rotational equilibrium" - a steady state where the expected magnitude and angular updates of weight vectors stabilize. The key insight is that weight decay causes neurons to reach an equilibrium where gradient updates (increasing weight norms) balance against weight decay (decreasing norms), resulting in consistent angular rotation rates across different network components. The authors demonstrate that this balanced rotation is crucial for effective training. They show how weight decay acts differently from traditional L2 regularization by creating two distinct "effective step sizes" - one for weight rotations (ηr) and another for bias updates (ηg). Their analysis reveals why techniques like AdamW outperform Adam with L2 regularization: AdamW produces balanced rotation across layers while Adam+L2 creates imbalanced rotation that depends on gradient magnitudes. The paper introduces "Rotational Variants" (RVs) of standard optimizers that directly control angular updates without relying on weight decay, demonstrating that explicit rotation control can achieve similar benefits while reducing the need for learning rate warmup.

title: A Study of BFLOAT16 for Deep Learning Training
author: Dhiraj Kalamkar et al
description: BFLOAT16 uses 8 bits for the exponent (same as FP32) and 7 bits for the mantissa, providing the same dynamic range as FP32 while using only 16 bits of storage. The key advantage of BFLOAT16 over IEEE FP16 is that it eliminates the need for loss scaling and hyperparameter tuning since it maintains FP32's dynamic range, avoiding the gradient underflow problems that plague FP16 training. The authors implemented an emulation library called Quantlib that converts FP32 tensors to BFLOAT16 precision by zeroing out the lower 16 bits and applying round-to-nearest-even rounding.

title: SGD with Large Step Sizes Learn Sparse Features
author: Maksym Adriuschenko et al
description: The authors identify that large step sizes cause "loss stabilization" - where the training loss plateaus at a high level while hidden beneficial dynamics occur in parameter space. They model this phenomenon using stochastic differential equations, showing that the combination of gradient descent with multiplicative noise (proportional to the neural tangent kernel features) drives networks toward sparser representations. The key insight is that SGD with large step sizes creates a three-phase training process: (1) initial loss reduction, (2) loss stabilization with hidden sparsification dynamics, and (3) final fitting when step size is decayed. During phase 2, the multiplicative noise structure biases the optimization toward solutions that minimize the norm of Jacobian columns, effectively learning sparse features. The authors validate this across multiple architectures from diagonal linear networks to deep CNNs, consistently observing that longer loss stabilization phases lead to better generalization and sparser learned features.

title: Understanding Decoupled and Early Weight Decay
author: Lukas Balles et al
description: This paper provides a novel decomposition of the Adam optimizer, arguing that it can be understood as combining two distinct mechanisms: taking the sign of gradients (determining update direction) and variance adaptation (determining update magnitude based on gradient reliability). The authors demonstrate that the sign aspect dominates Adam's behavior and is responsible for both its successes and its reported generalization problems, while variance adaptation can be beneficially applied to other optimizers like SGD. Through theoretical analysis on quadratic problems and experiments across multiple domains, they show that sign-based methods work best for noisy, ill-conditioned, axis-aligned problems, and introduce M-SVAG (momentum stochastic variance-adapted gradient) as an alternative that applies variance adaptation without the potentially harmful sign component. The work challenges the conventional understanding of adaptive optimizers by isolating these two mechanisms and showing that generalization issues stem specifically from the sign aspect rather than adaptivity itself.

title: Why Do We Need Weight Decay in Modern Deep Learning?
author: Francesco D'Angelo et al
description: This paper examines why weight decay is so widely used in modern deep learning, arguing that its role differs significantly from traditional regularization theory. The authors demonstrate that weight decay serves different purposes in over-training versus under-training regimes: in over-training scenarios (like ResNets on vision tasks), it enhances SGD's implicit regularization through loss stabilization, while in under-training regimes (like LLM training), it improves optimization by modulating the bias-variance tradeoff and ensuring training stability. The research challenges the conventional view of weight decay as primarily an explicit regularizer, instead showing that it beneficially modifies training dynamics across different learning scenarios. The work provides both empirical evidence and theoretical insights to explain weight decay's effectiveness, concluding that it's valuable not for direct regularization but for creating more favorable optimization trajectories.

title: Simple linear attention language models balance the recall-throughput tradeoff
author: Arora et al
description: This paper introduces BASED, a language model architecture that combines linear attention (using a 2nd-order Taylor approximation of softmax) with small sliding window attention (64-128 tokens) to balance recall ability and computational efficiency. The authors demonstrate through theory and experiments that there exists a fundamental tradeoff between a model's memory consumption during inference and its capacity to perform associative recall tasks. BASED expands the Pareto frontier of this tradeoff by using linear attention for global context and precise sliding window attention for local token interactions, achieving up to 24× higher throughput than FlashAttention-2 while maintaining competitive recall performance. The work includes custom IO-aware CUDA implementations that make the hybrid architecture practically efficient, addressing the gap between theoretical and real-world performance of linear attention methods.

title: Just Read Twice: Closing the Recall Gap for Recurrent Language Models
author: Simran Arora et al
description: Just Read Twice introduces two complementary approaches to address recurrent language models' brittleness in recall-intensive tasks by exploiting data ordering effects. The first innovation, JRT-Prompt, simply repeats context information twice in the input, allowing the model to see all possible data orders and make better memory selection decisions during the second pass. The second innovation, JRT-RNN, uses a non-causal encoder-decoder architecture with "Prefix Linear Attention" that processes prompts bidirectionally while maintaining causal decoding, combined with a mixed training objective using both next-token prediction and masked language modeling. The key theoretical insight is formalizing how recall difficulty reduces to the set disjointness problem from communication complexity, showing that memory requirements depend critically on which set (smaller vs larger) appears first in the sequence, and that non-causal processing can achieve optimal O(min(|A|,|B|)) space complexity regardless of ordering.

title: Gated Delta Networks: Improving Mamba2 With Delta Rule
author: Songlin Yang et al
description: Gated Delta Networks introduces a unified "gated delta rule" that combines two previously separate memory management mechanisms - gating (for rapid memory erasure) and the delta rule (for precise memory updates) - into a single framework that enables both adaptive forgetting and targeted memory modifications. The key technical contribution is extending the hardware-efficient chunkwise parallel training algorithm to support this combined approach, preserving the computational benefits while gaining the complementary strengths of both mechanisms. Unlike Mamba2 which uniformly decays all memory associations or DeltaNet which can only update one key-value pair at a time, Gated DeltaNet can selectively clear irrelevant information while precisely updating specific associations based on the gating parameter α. The paper also develops hybrid architectures that strategically combine Gated DeltaNet layers with sliding window attention or Mamba2 layers, achieving better performance than pure recurrent models while maintaining training efficiency.

title: Bridging the Gap for Tokenizer-Free Language Models
author: Dokook Choe et al
description: This paper demonstrates that purely character-based language models can achieve competitive performance with word-based models on large-scale datasets, contrary to the prevailing assumption that tokenizers are essential for state-of-the-art results. The authors train a vanilla 40-layer transformer with 836M parameters on raw UTF-8 bytes using the One Billion Word benchmark, achieving a perplexity of 23.0 that matches contemporary word-based models (ranging from 21.8 to 28.0). The model processes text without any preprocessing, tokenization, or explicit word boundary information, making it more general and suitable for morphologically complex languages while avoiding the complexity of language-dependent preprocessing steps. Through analysis of intermediate representations, the authors show that different transformer layers capture word-level semantic information at varying depths, with earlier layers learning general linguistic features and later layers specializing for character prediction tasks.

title: Byte Latent Transformer: Patches Scale Better Than Tokens
author: Artidoro Pagnoni et al
description: The paper introduces the Byte Latent Transformer (BLT), a new tokenizer-free architecture that operates directly on raw bytes and, for the first time, matches the performance of tokenization-based models like LLaMA 3 at scale (up to 8B parameters and 4T training bytes) while offering up to 50% inference efficiency gains. BLT uses dynamic entropy-based patching to group bytes into variable-length patches based on prediction difficulty, allowing the model to allocate more compute where data complexity demands it, unlike fixed tokenization schemes. The architecture introduces a new scaling dimension where both model size and patch size can be increased simultaneously while maintaining the same inference budget, demonstrating superior scaling trends compared to token-based models in fixed-inference FLOP experiments. BLT shows significant improvements in robustness to noisy inputs and character-level understanding tasks, outperforming tokenization-based models on orthographic knowledge, phonology, and low-resource machine translation while maintaining competitive performance on standard benchmarks.

title: Byte Latent Transformer: Patches Scale Better Than Tokens
author: Artidoro Pagnoni et al
description: The paper introduces the Byte Latent Transformer (BLT), a new tokenizer-free architecture that operates directly on raw bytes and, for the first time, matches the performance of tokenization-based models like LLaMA 3 at scale (up to 8B parameters and 4T training bytes) while offering up to 50% inference efficiency gains. BLT uses dynamic entropy-based patching to group bytes into variable-length patches based on prediction difficulty, allowing the model to allocate more compute where data complexity demands it, unlike fixed tokenization schemes. The architecture introduces a new scaling dimension where both model size and patch size can be increased simultaneously while maintaining the same inference budget, demonstrating superior scaling trends compared to token-based models in fixed-inference FLOP experiments. BLT shows significant improvements in robustness to noisy inputs and character-level understanding tasks, outperforming tokenization-based models on orthographic knowledge, phonology, and low-resource machine translation while maintaining competitive performance on standard benchmarks.

title: Train for the Worst, Plan for the Best: Understaning Token Ordering in Masked Diffusion Models
author: Jaeyeon Kim et al
description: This paper examines the trade-off between training complexity and inference flexibility in Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) compared to autoregressive models (ARMs). The key novelty is demonstrating that while MDMs must learn exponentially more difficult masking subproblems during training (many of which are computationally intractable), they can strategically avoid these hard problems at inference time through adaptive token ordering strategies. The authors prove that for certain benign data distributions, a large fraction of MDM's training subproblems are computationally hard, yet show that simple adaptive inference methods like "Top-K probability margin" can sidestep these difficulties without retraining. On logic puzzles like Sudoku, this approach dramatically improves MDM performance from <7% to ~90% accuracy, even outperforming autoregressive models that were explicitly trained with supervised teacher forcing to learn the correct decoding order.

title: Highly Compressed Tokenizer Can Generate Without Training
author: Lukas Lao Beyer et al
description: This paper demonstrates that highly compressed 1D image tokenizers like TiTok can generate images without training any dedicated generative model, using only gradient-based test-time optimization of tokens. The key novelty is showing that extreme compression (down to just 32 discrete tokens per image) creates a highly semantic latent space where simple token manipulations enable sophisticated image editing - for example, copying a single token between images can transfer global attributes like lighting or blur. The authors introduce a gradient-based framework that optimizes tokens directly using plug-and-play objectives like CLIP similarity for text-guided editing or reconstruction loss for inpainting, achieving competitive generation quality (FID of 8.2) compared to methods that require training full generative models. This work reveals that the tokenizer's decoder in highly compressed representations essentially functions as a generative model itself, enabling training-free image generation through straightforward optimization in the compact token space.

title: Highly Compressed Tokenizer Can Generate Without Training
author: Lukas Lao Beyer et al
description: This paper demonstrates that highly compressed 1D image tokenizers like TiTok can generate images without training any dedicated generative model, using only gradient-based test-time optimization of tokens. The key novelty is showing that extreme compression (down to just 32 discrete tokens per image) creates a highly semantic latent space where simple token manipulations enable sophisticated image editing - for example, copying a single token between images can transfer global attributes like lighting or blur. The authors introduce a gradient-based framework that optimizes tokens directly using plug-and-play objectives like CLIP similarity for text-guided editing or reconstruction loss for inpainting, achieving competitive generation quality (FID of 8.2) compared to methods that require training full generative models. This work reveals that the tokenizer's decoder in highly compressed representations essentially functions as a generative model itself, enabling training-free image generation through straightforward optimization in the compact token space.

title: Deep Researcher with Test-Time Diffusion
author: Rujun Han et al
description: The paper introduces Test-Time Diffusion Deep Researcher (TTD-DR), a novel framework that conceptualizes research report generation as a diffusion process, where an initial draft is iteratively refined through a "denoising" mechanism guided by retrieval of external information. The framework incorporates two key mechanisms: report-level refinement via denoising with retrieval, where the entire draft evolves dynamically, and component-wise optimization via self-evolution, which enhances the quality of each step in the research workflow. TTD-DR addresses limitations of existing deep research agents by maintaining global context and coherence throughout the research process, mimicking the iterative nature of human research through cycles of planning, drafting, searching, and revision.

title: KNN-Diffusion: Image Generation via Large-Scale Retrieval
author: Shelly Sheynin et al
description: The paper introduces kNN-Diffusion, a novel text-to-image generation model that leverages large-scale k-Nearest-Neighbors retrieval to train diffusion models without requiring paired text-image datasets, addressing the significant limitation of existing models that need extensive labeled data. The method conditions the diffusion process on both CLIP embeddings and k nearest neighbor image embeddings from a retrieval database, which helps bridge the distribution gap between text and image embeddings while enabling the model to learn from actual samples in the target distribution.

title: DiffusionBERT: Improving Generative Masked Language Models with Diffusion Models
author: Zhengfu He et al
description: This paper introduces DiffusionBERT, which combines discrete diffusion models with pre-trained language models like BERT for text generation. The authors propose using BERT to learn the reverse diffusion process, where text is gradually denoised from a fully masked state back to coherent text, leveraging the shared denoising objective between diffusion models and masked language models. They introduce a novel "spindle" noise schedule that masks tokens based on their information content (high-information tokens first) and demonstrate that "time-agnostic decoding" works best, where the model doesn't explicitly use time step information during generation.
